questions.question: What core problem do agent economies address?
options: How to make agents run faster

Agents produce value jointly but must be rewarded individually — equal splits and last-contributor-takes-all are unfair or gameable, so credit attribution, mechanism design, and reputation become the problem family
How to tax AI systems
How to make agents speak the same language
questions.explanation: Shapley values are fair by construction but expensive (N! permutations); the 2025-2026 literature pushes practical approximations: Shapley sampling, monotone-aggregation auctions, and DID-bound on-chain reputation.

questions.question: What are the five layers of the agent-economy reference stack?
options: Hardware, OS, runtime, app, UI
DePIN (physical compute) → Identity (W3C DIDs + reputation) → Cognition (LLM + RAG + MCP) → Settlement (account abstraction) → Governance (Agentic DAOs)
Mine, validate, stake, vote, govern
Train, deploy, monitor, scale, retire
questions.explanation: It's a reference map, not a requirement — Bittensor uses layers 1-2 and parts of 3-4 with no governance layer; OpenAI agents use essentially only cognition. DIDs make reputation portable across platforms.

questions.question: How does Shapley-value credit attribution work, and why is sampling needed in practice?
options: Each agent self-reports its contribution

Average each agent's marginal contribution across all orderings of the coalition — unique allocation satisfying efficiency, symmetry, linearity, and null axioms; but N=10 means 3.6M permutations, so you sample orderings
Split rewards by tokens consumed
The newest agent gets the largest share
questions.explanation: For each permutation, record what agent i adds when joining the agents before it; average over permutations. AAMAS 2025's decentralized LaMAS applies exactly this to reward contributing agents fairly.

questions.question: Why does a second-price token auction make agents bid truthfully?
options: Lying is detected by a classifier
The winner pays the SECOND-highest value, so under monotone aggregation your bid only determines whether you win, not what you pay — misreporting can only hurt you
Bids are encrypted
All agents share the same value function
questions.explanation: Google Research's mechanism-design work applies the classic Vickrey insight to LLM output aggregation: outsource completions to differently-priced agents, pick the best, pay fairly, and no one gains by misreporting.

questions.question: How do Bittensor, Fetch.ai/ASI, and Gonka differ in what they pay for?
options: All three pay per kilowatt-hour

Bittensor pays for task-specific output quality (validator-ranked subnet rewards), Fetch pays for utility measured by paying users (FET for inference), Gonka pays for verifiable transformer inference as resource-productive proof-of-work
They are three names for one network
All three pay only in US dollars
questions.explanation: The shared economic lesson: pay for output quality or verified useful work, not raw compute consumed. All three are production-grade as of April 2026.
